hero.kicker: Open source, real time generative images
hero.sub: potocolom turns your canvas into finished images while you sketch, at 2 to 4 frames per second. Run it on your own GPU for free, or let the cloud carry the weight.
features.title: Built for both worlds
features.sub: One codebase behind everything: the free install on your desk and the managed cloud are the same application.
features.f1_title: A live loop, not a queue
features.f1_body: Canvas frames go up, generated frames come down, over one WebSocket. Drawing feels like drawing.
features.f2_body: Self-host with docker compose on any NVIDIA or AMD card, or subscribe and draw on a rented fleet.
features.f3_body: Drop a model manifest and the interface adapts to its parameters. No frontend release required.
features.f4_body: No public gallery. Your images live behind signed links you can share and revoke, and your data exports in one click.
gallery.sub: Sketches become finished images in real time. A sample of work from the community - the kind of output the canvas produces.
gallery.prompts_hint: Hover to pause. Click any prompt to copy it.
split.title: Free where it should be, paid where it must be
split.cloud_p1: No GPU needed, drawing in seconds
split.cloud_p3: The same application, operated for you
fork.title: Take the repo. Run it on your GPU.
fork.cmd2: cd potocolom && make setup && make deps
fork.out2: Virtualenvs built. PostgreSQL, Redis and MinIO listening.
fork.b1: The realtime drawing loop is in the repo: strokes up, renders down, on your hardware.
fork.b2: New models arrive as manifests. The interface reads their schema - no waiting on a release.
fork.b3: One compose file on NVIDIA or AMD. No account required, no public gallery, no phone-home.
footer.tagline: One AGPL codebase, self-hosted or in the cloud.
pricing.title: Simple tiers, honest credits
pricing.sub: Credits meter GPU seconds transparently: drawing drains them live, generations cost a handful each. Beta pricing, subject to change before public launch - the waitlist hears first.
pricing.t1_b3: Or a few thousand quick generations
pricing.trial: Every beta invite includes a one-time trial grant - no card needed to try.
bench.title: Model runtime on {vram} GB VRAM
bench.sub: Curated prompts, fixed parameter variants, one model loaded at a time. GPU milliseconds are measured on the worker; wall time includes queue and I/O.
bench.empty_body: Run make benchmark on a GPU worker, then make benchmark-publish to copy results.json into the site.
bench.summary_note: Load time is paid once per model. GPU time is denoising only. Wall time is end-to-end per image.
bench.by_prompt_note: Average GPU time across variants for each curated prompt.
bench.reference_note: Models marked reference are benchmark timings only. They are not offered in the app because of upstream license revenue caps.
bench.specs_note: Hardware and license requirements from worker manifests. VRAM is for full GPU residency; lower-VRAM cards may use CPU offload at reduced speed.
bench.charts_note: Ranked by GPU denoise time. Toggle metrics on the bar chart; expand a model below for per-prompt breakdown.
bench.leaderboard_note: Models ranked fastest to slowest by average GPU denoise time on this hardware.
bench.chart_bars_desc: Horizontal bars for the selected metric.
bench.chart_grouped_desc: Denoise time compared with end-to-end latency per model.
bench.chart_category_desc: Radar chart of average GPU denoising time per prompt category. Further from the center is faster on each axis.
bench.chart_radar_note: Each axis is a prompt category. Hover axes, labels, or points for exact GPU times.
bench.chart_heatmap_desc: GPU denoising time per model and category. Cell tint shows relative speed within this table.
bench.details_note: Expand a model for variant and prompt tables.
wp.title: How potocolom works
wp.sub: What happens between a stroke on the canvas and the pixels coming back: the platform's anatomy, the realtime loop, GPU scheduling, the model system, and the safety, privacy and billing machinery. Everything on this page is specified in the public docs and recorded as decisions.
wp.s1_title: A live brush, not a prompt queue
wp.s1_p1: potocolom turns an image model into a drawing partner. You sketch on a canvas and a diffusion model re-renders your strokes as a finished image while you draw, several times per second - not once, after a button press. The prompt sets the scene; your strokes decide the composition.
wp.s1_p2: Around that loop sits a full studio: classic prompt-to-image generation with per-model controls, a history of everything you make, and a gallery to pin the keepers. This page explains the machinery underneath, in the order a stroke meets it.
wp.s2_p1: Three moving parts. The studio runs in your browser. The platform API is a stateless relay and scheduler: it owns no GPU and never touches image bytes; it routes frames, dispatches work and keeps the authoritative record of every job and asset in PostgreSQL. GPU workers hold the models and do the actual rendering.
wp.s2_p2: Workers always dial out to the platform over one persistent WebSocket. Because the connection starts on the worker's side, a GPU machine needs no inbound ports, no public address and no firewall exceptions - any machine that can open a connection can serve, from a data-center card to the desktop under your desk.
wp.s2_p3: On connect, a worker announces its models in a manifest: capabilities, parameter schema, VRAM needs. The platform validates every request against that schema, picks a worker with the model warm, and streams progress back live. Finished images never pass through the API: workers upload them straight to storage with signed URLs, and your browser loads them the same way. If the platform restarts, it rebuilds its queue from the database and carries on.
wp.fig_arch_cap: The three moving parts. Frames flow through the relay; images travel directly between workers, storage and your browser.
wp.s3_p1: While you draw, the canvas is snapshotted and sent as a compact binary frame - a small header plus WebP pixels - on the same WebSocket that brings results back. A distilled diffusion model re-renders it in a handful of denoising steps at 512 px, and generated frames stream back at 2 to 4 per second.
wp.s3_p2: The loop never queues stale input: if a new stroke arrives while the GPU is busy, it replaces the pending frame rather than lining up behind it. Latest input wins; superseded frames are simply dropped. You always see the newest state of your drawing, never a replay of where it was.
wp.s3_p3: Sessions are admitted against fixed per-worker slots and never oversubscribed. Go idle for about a minute and your slot is released - the canvas stays in the browser, and the next stroke reacquires one, usually instantly. If the machine under your session vanishes, the platform reassigns you, the canvas is re-sent, and drawing continues.
wp.fig_loop_cap: Admission, the frame loop, idle release and worker-loss recovery.
wp.s4_p1: GPU seconds are the scarce resource, so scheduling is specified rather than improvised. Realtime sessions come first and are never oversubscribed. Queued generations fill whatever capacity is idle, and are preempted between denoising steps when a drawing session needs the slot; nothing ever preempts an active session.
wp.s4_p2: How many sessions one GPU can hold is measured, not guessed: at warmup a worker benchmarks its models and advertises the concurrency it can sustain while keeping frame pacing above the bar.
wp.s4_p3: Model placement follows demand. A hot set of fast models stays pinned in VRAM for instant sessions; everything else loads on demand, with load times measured and reported. When the pool is genuinely full, new sessions wait in an admission queue with a live position - and that queue is exactly the signal that brings more GPUs online.
wp.s5_p1: A model, to the platform, is a JSON manifest: what it can do, how much VRAM it needs, and a parameters block that is standard JSON Schema. That schema is the single source of truth - the studio renders its controls from it and the platform validates every request against it - so adding a model to a fleet makes it usable everywhere, with the right knobs, without touching a line of frontend code.
wp.s5_p2: Manifests also carry the recipe: distilled fast models (the Turbo, Lightning and LCM class) for the drawing loop, heavier samplers for quality renders, optional scheduler overrides and fused acceleration LoRAs. On a single consumer 16 GB card, once warm, the fast class renders a 512 px image in about a third of a second, the Lightning class takes a few seconds at 1024 px, and the quality class about twenty seconds. The numbers are not marketing: they come from a benchmark harness anyone can run, and the results are published on the live benchmark page.
wp.s5_p3: Models ship only after their licenses are vetted. Usage terms, attribution requirements and commercial limits are recorded in the manifest itself, and required attributions are honored in the product.
wp.s6_p1: Everything is WebP end to end - canvas frames, generated frames, stored assets - chosen for quality per byte at interactive speeds. Every generation stores its parameters and seed next to the image, so any result can be reproduced exactly, and provenance is recorded in the schema so an image can tell where it came from.
wp.s6_p2: Assets are private by default and delivered through short-lived signed URLs; sharing is an explicit act. Nothing you create is used to train models.
wp.s6_p3: The hosted service screens prompts through a layered pipeline - normalization, then rules, then a classifier - on every path a prompt can take, including live drawing. Enforcement works on strikes, without retaining prompt text.
wp.s7_p1: The cloud meters usage in credits, and a credit corresponds to GPU time actually spent. Before any GPU second is spent, the platform reserves an estimate; when the work finishes, it commits the actual amount; failed work refunds automatically. Drawing sessions meter in small chunks only while active - an idle canvas costs nothing.
wp.s7_p2: Every money path tolerates replay. Payment webhooks deduplicate on event ids, the credit ledger is append-only with unique source keys, and reservations are idempotent with a timeout that returns stranded credits on its own. Retries and redeliveries are no-ops by construction.
wp.s7_p3: Payment surfaces are hosted by the payment provider, so card data never touches platform servers. If billing is ever unreachable, reserving fails closed - no GPU time is granted on credit - while finished work settles through a retry queue until it lands exactly once.
wp.fig_credits_cap: Money in, the append-only ledger, and money out through the quota contract.
wp.s8_p1: GPU machines vanish; the design treats that as normal. A worker dying mid-generation gets the job requeued exactly once before it fails visibly - an input that crashes workers cannot burn GPU time in a loop. A worker dying mid-session triggers a transparent resume: another machine takes over and your canvas is re-sent.
wp.s8_p2: The platform restarting rebuilds its queue from the database. The database being down degrades the service instead of stopping it: live drawing keeps working while history waits. Failures refund, retries are bounded, and no failure mode produces a surprise bill.
wp.fig_failures_cap: Every failure, what you see, and what the money does.
wp.s9_p1: The product is AGPL 3.0 and complete on its own: the code that serves you - including this page - is the code in the repository, and you can run the entire platform on your own GPU.
wp.s9_p2: The design is public too: the architecture documents, the wire protocol, the benchmark methodology, and a decision ledger that records not only every choice but the alternatives that were rejected and why.
wp.cta_docs: Read the full docs on GitHub
app.title: Studio
app.draw_placeholder: The drawing tool arrives with issue #3. Strokes on the left, the live render on the right.
app.generate_placeholder: Prompt-to-image generation with history arrives with issues #2 and #16.
app.edit_placeholder: Image editing joins after the drawing tool ships.
app.enhance_placeholder: One-click enhance routes a draft to a heavier model. It lands with model routing.
app.gen.title: Generate
app.gen.sub: Prompt to image on your connected worker.
app.gen.prompt_placeholder: a castle on a hill at sunset, oil painting
app.gen.working_suffix: in the queue or rendering; results appear as they finish
app.gen.no_models: No models registered. Start a worker and reload: it announces its models to the API when it connects.
app.upscale.title: Upscale
app.upscale.sub: Enlarge a finished image with a pixel upscaler.
app.upscale.need_image: Select a finished image in the history strip to upscale.
app.upscale.no_models: No upscale models registered. Start a worker that announces an upscale-capable model.
app.metrics.title: Metrics
app.metrics.sub: Session usage timelines and published benchmark runs.
app.metrics.by_model_desc: Average GPU denoise time per model in this session.
app.metrics.model_activity_desc: Denoise windows per model in the selected range.
app.metrics.job_status_desc: Completed, running, and queued jobs in the current history window.
app.metrics.vram_unavailable: VRAM needs a connected ROCm/CUDA worker
app.metrics.runtime_empty: Generate an image to see runtime metrics here.
app.metrics.benchmark_chart_desc: Average GPU denoise time compared with end-to-end wall time for the selected run.
app.metrics.benchmark_timeline_desc: GPU time per image over the run. Gaps between models are load time; the report has no hardware GPU or VRAM samples.
app.metrics.benchmark_empty: No published benchmark results yet. Run make benchmark, then make benchmark-publish.
app.metrics.gpu_timeline_empty: Generate an image to populate the timeline.
app.metrics.gpu_timeline_hint: Compute and queue lanes sample every 2 s from active jobs. Model lanes show denoise windows from this session.
app.metrics.gpu_timeline_hint_hw: GPU % and VRAM % sample every 2 s from the worker (rocm-smi / NVML). Model lanes show denoise windows from this session.
app.metrics.range_requires_persistence: Requires persisted metrics (coming in Phase A).
wl.title: Be first on the canvas
wl.sub: The cloud opens as an invite-only beta. Join the waitlist and you will hear exactly twice: at the self-host launch and when your invite is ready.
wl.done: You are on the list. Talk soon.
wl.already: That address is already on the list.
wl.error: That did not work - mind trying again?
wl.privacy_note: One address, one purpose, deletable on request.
error.message: Nothing lives at this address. The canvas, however, is very much alive.
legal.title: Legal notice
legal.operator_body: This website and the potocolom project are operated by Leon Elliott Fuller. Contact: admin@leonfuller.com.
legal.purpose_body: potocolom.leonfuller.com presents the open source potocolom project and collects waitlist signups for its upcoming cloud service. Nothing is sold on this site today; this notice will be extended with full identification details before the paid service opens.
legal.law_body: This notice is provided pursuant to Spain's Law 34/2002 on information society services (LSSI-CE). The service operates from Spain; Spanish and European Union law apply.
privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.intro: The short version: this site sets no cookies, runs no third-party trackers, and collects nothing about you except an email address you choose to give us.
privacy.waitlist_body: If you join the waitlist we store your email address and interface language, and use them for exactly one thing: contacting you about the potocolom launch and beta. No newsletters beyond that, no sharing, no profiling. One email to admin@leonfuller.com and the address is deleted.
privacy.cookies_body: No cookies are set by this site. Traffic statistics, if collected at all, come from server-side aggregate counts with no client-side beacon and no personal data.
privacy.processors_body: The site and waitlist run on Cloudflare infrastructure, which acts as a processor. When the cloud service launches it will carry its own privacy policy, including the GPU providers it uses.
privacy.rights_body: The GDPR applies in full: access, rectification, deletion and portability. Write to admin@leonfuller.com. Data controller: Leon Elliott Fuller.
